topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a saccharine wave of joy washing over the reception hall, and I felt a familiar, hot pressure building behind my eyes. Then I saw him. Standing near the cake table, laughing with the groom’s father, was Liam. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that did nothing to calm the churning in my gut. Every muscle in my jaw tightened until it ached. I watched him move, the easy familiarity of his posture sending a jolt of pure, corrosive anger through me. I forced a smile, a brittle, painful thing that felt like cracking glass on my face. When he finally turned, our eyes met across the crowded room. The casual warmth in his gaze hit me like a physical blow, and I took a deliberate step backward, my knuckles white where I gripped my champagne flute. I needed air, needed distance, needed to dismantle the entire scene.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," I muttered under my breath, the words tasting like ash. My hands were shaking so badly I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. Across the room, amidst the glittering streamers and the forced merriment, there he was—Mark. He looked… happy. Too happy. The sight of him, so effortlessly integrated into this celebration, ignited a furious heat deep in my chest. I could feel the blood pounding a frantic rhythm against my ribs. I steered myself toward the edge of the patio, needing the cool night air to counteract the internal furnace. When he spotted me, his smile faltered for a split second, replaced by a flicker of something I couldn't quite decipher, but it was enough. I didn't stop walking; I just accelerated, the sound of my own footsteps a harsh counterpoint to the celebratory din.

[story 3]

I kept my gaze fixed on the floral arrangements, counting the petals, anything to avoid the inevitable collision. But the moment he turned toward my section of the room, the air seemed to thin out. Liam. He was talking animatedly to Sarah, his arm casually draped over her shoulder. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, something raw and ugly that I instantly regretted. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, and I could feel a distinct, burning sensation spreading up my neck. I watched his profile, cataloging every detail—the way his hair fell, the unguarded ease in his expression—and a sudden, blinding need to shatter the illusion hit me. I took a long, slow sip of water, my throat suddenly dry, and then I turned sharply, walking directly toward the exit, my pace deliberate, heavy with unspoken fury.
